# Episode 9: Containers in Research Workflows: Reproducibility and Granularity {.nonincremental}

::: {.callout-tip icon="false" .nonincremental}
## Questions

- How can I use container images to make my research more reproducible?
- How do I incorporate containers into my research workflow?
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false" .nonincremental}
## Objectives

- Understand how container images can help make research more reproducible.
- Understand what practical steps I can take to improve the reproducibility of my research using containers.
:::

---

## Bringing It Together: The Reproducibility Focus

So far, we focused mostly on Docker mechanics.
This section focuses on reproducibility in research workflows.

::: {.callout-note}

## Work in Progress...

- Reproducibility practices for containers are still evolving.
- The principles here are useful now, but best practice will continue to change.

:::

## Reproducibility

Reproducibility means:

- someone else (or future you) can rerun your computational work later
- results are as close as possible to the original
- exact same hardware is not required

Why this matters:

- research outputs increasingly depend on software
- software stacks are complex and dependency-heavy
- others need to verify and build on published work

---

## Why Containers Help Reproducibility

Containers are useful because they let you:

- run the same workflow across Windows, macOS, and Linux
- capture the exact software versions and dependencies
- preserve older software environments for legacy workflows
- optionally bundle key reference data with the environment
- archive a reusable snapshot that others can inspect and rerun

---

## Sharing Images

Docker Hub is useful for day-to-day sharing and collaboration.

But it is not ideal for long-term research archiving because:

- free accounts have a limit on how long a container image will be hosted if it is not updated
- it does not support adding persistent identifiers to container images
- it is easy to overwrite tagged container images with newer versions by mistake.

---

## Archiving and Persistently Identifying Container Images Using Zenodo

For publications, archive images in an immutable, citable form.
[Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) is one option.

Zenodo accepts tar archives.
Export an image with `docker image save`:

```bash
docker image save alice/alpine-python:v1 -o alpine-python.tar
```

If needed, compress before upload:

```bash
gzip alpine-python.tar
```

Note: Zenodo has upload size limits (for example, 50 GB per upload at the time of writing), so compression (gzip/zip) may be required.

---

## Why Archive with Zenodo?

After creating the archive, [deposit it on Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/deposit/) to:

- create a long-term archive snapshot that others can download and reuse
- mint a persistent DOI (*Digital Object Identifier*) for citation in publications
- support attribution and credit for your computational work

You will also provide metadata about the image and related work.

Zenodo is one option; some institutions provide equivalent archive/DOI services.

---

## Container Granularity

An important design choice is container **granularity**.

Two extremes are:

- create one large image with all tools for the full workflow
- create many small images, each for one command or workflow step

Most real workflows use a middle ground between these extremes.


---

## Positives and Negatives

::: {.callout-note .challenge-callout icon="false" }

## Positives and Negatives

What are the advantages and disadvantages of the two approaches to container granularity for research
workflows described above? Think about this
and write a few bullet points for advantages and disadvantages for each approach in the course Etherpad.

:::


---

## Solution

::: {.callout-note .solution-callout icon="false"  collapse="true"}

## Solution

This is not an exhaustive list but some of the advantages and disadvantages could be:

### Single Large Container Image

- Advantages:
  - Simpler to document
  - Full set of requirements packaged in one place
  - Potentially easier to maintain (though could be opposite if working with large, distributed group)
- Disadvantages:
  - Could get very large in size, making it more difficult to distribute
    - Could use [Docker multi-stage build](https://docs.docker.com/develop/develop-images/multistage-build) to reduce size
  - May end up with same dependency issues within the container image from different software requirements
  - Potentially more complex to test
  - Less re-useable for different, but related, work

### Multiple Smaller Container Images

- Advantages:
  - Individual components can be re-used for different, but related, work
  - Individual parts are smaller in size making them easier to distribute
  - Avoid dependency issues between different pieces of software
  - Easier to test
- Disadvantage:
  - More difficult to document
  - Potentially more difficult to maintain (though could be easier if working with large, distributed group)
  - May end up with dependency issues between component container images if they get out of sync
    
    

:::
